coredata.dc:title: A sensitive electrochemiluminescence glucose biosensor based on graphene quantum dot prepared from graphene oxide sheets and hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Kaile
Nie, Fei
Luo, Kai
Zheng, Xiaohui
Zheng, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a size of <10nm were generated from graphene oxide sheets by exploiting the scissor cut effect after sono- and photo-chemical treatment. During this process, hydrogen peroxide was added as the only chemical reagent. A strong cathodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal was generated by applying a cyclic voltammetry scanning on glassy carbon electrode in a mixture of GQD and potassium persulfate. The ECL properties of GQD/K2S2O8 coreactant system were studied in detail and a possible mechanism was proposed which revealed that the ECL signal was mainly dependent on the presence of the reduction of GQD and dissolved oxygen. Furthermore, the ECL signal was found to be quenched by H2O2, a product of enzymatic oxidation of glucose. Therefore, an ECL glucose biosensor was prepared by decorating a film consisting of glucose oxidase, chitosan and GQD on a glassy carbon electrode. Under the optimized conditions, the ECL decreased linearly in the 1.2 to 120pmol·L−1 glucose concentration range, and the detection limit was as low as 0.3pmol·L−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Biosensor
Graphene quantum dot
H2O2
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQD based on sono- and photo‑hydrogen peroxide reaction
Establishment and characterization of the ECL glucose biosensor
Performance of the ECL glucose biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21305111), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (No. 2015M572589) and Scientific Research Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory (No. 15JS099).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the sono- and photo-reactor for GQD preparation.

The TEM for GQD prepared with the sono- and photo-H2O2 reaction at the UV irradiation time of 5min (A) and 25min (B) with the size distribution (inset); AFM height images of GO (C) and GQDs (D). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (E) and the Raman spectroscopy (F) for GQD at the UV irradiation time of 25min.
The CV (A) and ECL (B) profiles of the blank PBS solution (0.1M, pH7.4), GQD (0.5mg·mL−1), and GQD with H2O2 (0.1M) or K2S2O8 (0.1M) as coreactant, respectively.

The CV (A) and ECL (B) profiles of GQD (0.5mg·mL−1), K2S2O8 (0.1M), and the mixture of GQD and K2S2O8 in the PBS solution (0.1M, pH7.4), respectively. The internal illustration shows the change of GQD CV profile before and after oxygen was inlet.
The EIS of bare GCE, GQD/GCE, chitosan-GQD/GCE and GOx/chitosan-GQD/GCE performed in 5.0mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 0.2M KCl solution containing 0.2M KCl5.0mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6.
The CV (A) and ECL-potential curves (B) of bare GCE, GQD/GCE, chitosan-GQD/GCE and GOx/chitosan-GQD/GCE in PBS (0.1M, pH7.4) contained 0.1M K2S2O8.

The ECL (A) profiles induced by different concentration of glucose and the corresponding calibration plots (B) of the ECL inhibition ratio against the glucose concentrations in the range of 1.2–120.0pmol·L−1.
The possible mechanism of the glucose ECL sensor.
An overview on recently reported nanomaterial-based methods for determination of glucose.

head.ce:title: A sensitive electrochemiluminescence glucose biosensor based on graphene quantum dot prepared from graphene oxide sheets and hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Analytical Science/Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Northwest University, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710069, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Science/Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a size of <10nm were generated from graphene oxide sheets by exploiting the scissor cut effect after sono- and photo-chemical treatment. During this process, hydrogen peroxide was added as the only chemical reagent. A strong cathodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal was generated by applying a cyclic voltammetry scanning on glassy carbon electrode in a mixture of GQD and potassium persulfate. The ECL properties of GQD/K2S2O8 coreactant system were studied in detail and a possible mechanism was proposed which revealed that the ECL signal was mainly dependent on the presence of the reduction of GQD and dissolved oxygen. Furthermore, the ECL signal was found to be quenched by H2O2, a product of enzymatic oxidation of glucose. Therefore, an ECL glucose biosensor was prepared by decorating a film consisting of glucose oxidase, chitosan and GQD on a glassy carbon electrode. Under the optimized conditions, the ECL decreased linearly in the 1.2 to 120pmol·L−1 glucose concentration range, and the detection limit was as low as 0.3pmol·L−1.

Novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by cutting treatment of graphene oxide (GO) sheets with sono- and photo‑hydrogen peroxide system. A dramatic sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor for the glucose determination was achieved.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has attracted significant attention because it combines both the advantages of luminescence analysis and electrochemical assay, such as absence of background optical signal, easy of reaction control by applying electrode potential [1,2]. Therefore, this kind of method always shows high sensitivity, good selectivity and wide response range. Except for the traditional ECL luminophores such as Ru(bpy)3 2+, some nanomaterials including CdTe, ZnS and other composite nanomaterials have been reported to be used as ECL reagents [3–5]. In addition, some new luminescence reagents with novel luminescent properties can also be obtained by the combination of these nanomaterials [6,7]. Au and Ag nano clusters and some carbon nano materials like carbon dots and graphene oxide (GO) have been reported with ECL properties [8–11]. These kinds of ECL reagents consisted of singular elements have good application prospects due to low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and low environmental hazard.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as the zero-dimensional carbon-based quantum dots, have strong quantum confinement, edge effects and been assumed numerous novel performances [12] when their sizes are the infinite exaction Bohr diameter [13,14]. Recently, the potential application of GQD in many fields was discussed, such as photovoltaics [15–18], biomedicine [19,20], bioimaging [21–24], photoluminescence [25–27], photocatalysis [28], phototransistor [29,30], light-emitting diodes [31], thermal conductor [32] and sensors [33–36]. GQD were also found to exhibit ECL properties similar to others carbon dots and can be used as ECL luminophores. Zhu et al. reported a one-pot microwave-assisted approach for the preparation of greenish yellow-luminescent GQD (gGQD) and brightly blue-luminescent GQD (bGQD). ECL behaviors of the gGQD and bGQD were studied when K2S2O8 was used as coreactant like most of other quantum dots [37]. Chi et al. found a new cathodic ECL signal for single-layer GQD by using l-cysteine as coreactant [38]. They also prepared hydrazide modified GQD and found this kind of GQD produced a strong anodic ECL signal in alkaline solutions. The anodic ECL signal can be enhanced greatly by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) due to the coreaction effect [39]. As known, different preparation methods will lead to the size and functional groups of GQD differently. Therefore, the ECL properties of various GQD would also be different because the optical properties of GQD present size-dependent shape/edge-state variations [14]. Thus, it's necessary to provide a facile method for the preparation of size and morphology controlled GQD.

Determination of glucose is so important due to their essential significance for medical diagnostics that many efforts have been taken to monitor glucose concentrations in the human body. Among them, methods based on GOx are most popular for the highly sensitive and specific detection of glucose. Most of the reported optical or electrochemical glucose biosensors are based on the oxidation of glucose catalyzed by GOx during this process the products of H2O2 and gluconic acid were generated.

Herein, a sono- and photo‑hydrogen peroxide reaction for preparation of GQD from GO nanosheets in the neutral condition has been reported. During the GQD preparation experiment, hydrogen peroxide is the only chemical reagent being used. By simply adjusting the ultrasonic and UV irradiation time and the concentration of hydrogen peroxide, the morphology and properties of the obtained GQD can be controlled. Meanwhile, hydrogen peroxide was converted into water during the process, thus left no other impurities in the GQD preparation. Therefore, GQD prepared in this work can be used directly without subsequent treatment such as dialysis. This allows the method to be very simple and time-saving. The optical properties of the obtained GQD were studied. Furthermore, it was found that the as-prepared GQD was able to produce a cathodic ECL signal. The ECL intensity can be enhanced sensitively by K2S2O8 while H2O2 showed no coreaction effect on the ECL signal. Instead, when K2S2O8 was used as coreactant, the ECL signal was found to be inhibited significantly by H2O2. Furthermore, the ECL mechanism was discussed preliminarily. At last, an ECL glucose sensor was fabricated based on immobilization of glucose oxidase (GOx) and GQD, an ECL sensor for determination was proposed. The glucose ECL sensor showed a high sensitivity with the detection limit of 0.3pmol·L−1, which is obviously superior to that of other electrochemical or ECL methods [40–46].

Natural graphite powder (>99% purity, 40μm in diameter) were purchased from Shanghai Carbon Co., Ltd. (China). Glucose oxidase (GOx, MW. 64500) was purchased from Sigma and used without further purification. Glucose, hydrogen peroxide (30%, w/v) and K2S2O8 were of analytical-reagent grades and purchased from Sigma. Chitosan (MW~1×106, >90% deacetylation) was purchased from Shanghai Xiangsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (>18MΩ·cm) from a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system.

TEM images were obtained using a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200kV. AFM images were recorded on a Multimode & Microscope (Bruker, USA). The XPS was acquired on a PHI 5400 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PE, U.S.A.). UV–vis spectra was measure on a U-3310 UV–vis spectrophotometer with a 1.0cm quartz cell (Hitachi, Japan), Raman spectra were acquired on a Renishaw in Via Raman microspectrometer (inVia, Renishaw). Tecan infinite F50 multimode reader was used to perform the PL fluorescence spectra scan.

For the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) determinations, a model CHI660D Electrochemistry Workstation (ChenHua Instruments Co., Shanghai, China) was employed with a three-electrode system, where an Ag/AgCl electrode was served as reference electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the auxiliary electrode, and the prepared electrode as the working electrode. All the electrochemical experiments were conducted at room temperature.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and ECL curves were recorded simultaneously using an MPI-E ECL analyzer (Xi'an Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co. Ltd.). A transparent electrochemical cell was used during the experiments, including a bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 4mm in diameter, i.e., with an apparent area of 12.56mm2) as the working electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQD based on sono- and photo‑hydrogen peroxide reaction
ce:section.ce:para: GO was prepared from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [47]. The sample GO (0.05g) was refluxed in an aqueous nitric acid solution (up to 2.6M, 40mL) under 70°C for 24h. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the suspension was subjected to mild ultrasonication. Then the pH value was tuned to 7 with NaOH (1M) solution. The suspension was disrupted into small pieces using an ultrasonic crusher for 60min. The sample was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove the large fragments of GO.

The sono- and photo‑hydrogen peroxide reactions were carried out in a simple and homemade reactor. The reactor was mainly equipped with a mercury lamp (365nm, 1000W, Bilon, Shanghai, China) and an ultrasonic cleaner (KQ-300DE, Kunshan, China). The mercury lamp was placed above the GO solution with the distance of 10cm (Fig. 1 ). During the sono- and photo reactions, the UV irradiation time, ultrasonic time and the concentration of hydrogen peroxide solution were optimized respectively to obtain GQD with different sizes and functional groups. In a typical experiment, GO aqueous suspension (100mg·L−1, 150mL) contained with 20mL H2O2 solution (6%) was treated with ultrasonic for 10min. The sample solution was finally collected after UV irradiation for 25min. The product was directly applied to subsequent analysis.

Prior to be used, GCE was polished on a polishing cloth with alumina particles (0.3μm and 0.05μm diameter, respectively). Then the electrode was cleaned in ethanol and ultrapure water by ultrasonication, respectively. Firstly, 5μL of the mixture of chitosan solution (1.0%) and GQD solution (1mg·mL−1) was cast onto the GCE surface. A beaker was covered over the electrode assuring slow evaporation of water and formation of a uniform film on the electrode surface. Thereupon, the chitosan-GQD membrane modified electrode was immersed into 0.10M phosphate buffer (pH7.4) containing 10mg·mL−1 GOx for 24h. Then, the GOx/chitosan-GQD composite membrane modified electrode was constructed. After rinsed with water, it was stored at 4°C in a refrigerator when not used.

The change of the GQD morphology during the sono- and photo‑hydrogen peroxide reaction was investigated with transmission electron microscope (TEM). In Fig. 2A, GO with micron scale were divided into small segments with the average diameter of 500nm after a short period of ultrasound and ultraviolet irradiation. Fig. 2B shows TEM image and size distribution (inset) of the prepared GQD, exhibiting in the scale with the average diameter <10nm after the reaction continued for 25min. Fig. 2C and D shows the AFM images of GO and GQD, respectively. The GO, as raw material, were non-uniform with a size distribution from several hundred nanometers to a few microns. The GQD obtained from the sono- and photo‑hydrogen peroxide reaction were of uniform size and had a diameter distribution <10nm. The heights of GO and GQD were both mainly distributed in the range of 0.5–2nm with an average height of 1.2nm, suggesting that most of GO and GQD have 1–2 graphene layers. XPS analysis was carried out to determine the chemical composition of GQD. Fig. 2E clearly shows four kinds of C 1s characterization peaks corresponding to CC/CC (peak 1, 284.5eV), CO (peak 2, 286.4eV), CO (peak 3, 287.8eV) and COOH (peak 4, 288.5eV), respectively. This is consistent with the C 1s characteristic peaks of the GQD reported previously. Raman spectra were also performed to provide convincing evidence for the structure of GQD (Fig. 2F). The peaks centered at 1360 and 1575cm−1 were attributed to the D and G bands, which are related to the sp2-bonded C atoms in the graphene structure and disordered C atoms at the edges of the GQD, respectively. It was observed that the ID/IG ratio of GQD was significantly higher than that of GO, which was due to the oxygen-rich edges of the GQD. In the UV spectrum, GQD shows a weak absorption peak at about 300nm. Also, a yellow fluorescence can be seen under the irradiation of ultraviolet lamp (see Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information). Like most semiconductor quantum dots, the photoluminescence emission wavelengths of the generated GQD depended strongly on the excitation wavelength. It was found that the emission wavelength of GQD had a tendency of red shift from 550nm to 625nm while the excitation wavelength was scanning from 325nm to 525nm (see Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information). Interestingly it was found, when we adjust the sono- and photo‑hydrogen peroxide reaction conditions, such as the reaction time or the concentration of hydrogen peroxide, the size of the obtained GQD and its luminescence properties were also changed with some regulations. This was not discussed in this work in detail.

The ECL properties of GQD were studied. As shown in Fig. 3A, when the potential was scanned from 0V to −1.6V, GQD (0.5mg·mL−1) produced nearly no ECL response (Fig. 3B) compared with that in blank PBS solution (0.10M, pH7.4). Therefore, it is necessary to select a suitable coreactant to enhance the ECL signal. K2S2O8 and H2O2 which was generally used as the coreactant in the quantum dots ECL study were selected and their enhancement effects on the GQD ECL signal was investigated, respectively. In Fig. 3B, it was shown that when H2O2 was added in PBS solution (0.10M, pH7.4) containing GQD alone, H2O2 can't significantly increase the GQD emission signal. However, an intensive cathodic ECL signal was observed when K2S2O8 alone was added in the same way. Therefore, K2S2O8 was used as the coreactant.

The effect of pH on the GQD-K2S2O8 ECL system was investigated. The results showed that the ECL signal changed little with the different pH (4.0–9.0), and the ECL signal was strongest when pH is 7.4. Considering the determination of glucose mostly occurred in the physiological environment, PBS buffer solution (pH7.4) was used in this work. (See Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information).
The ECL mechanism of the GQD-K2S2O8 system was studied in detail below.

The ECL properties of the GQD-K2S2O8 system were studied further. Firstly, the ECL signals were observed using GCE electrode in PBS solution (0.1M, pH7.4) with deoxygenating. As shown in Fig. 4A and B, CV and ECL curves of the interaction of GQD and K2S2O8 were given, respectively. When GQD (0.5mg·mL−1) was present alone, GQD produced nearly no ECL response with the potential scanned from 0V to −1.6V. When K2S2O8 was present alone, K2S2O8 gave a distinct irreversible cathodic reduction peak at about −0.8V and a weak ECL signal was also observed (at −1.6V, with an onset potential at about −1.2V). It indicated that the electrochemical reduction process of K2S2O8 was performed accompanied with a weak ECL emission. When K2S2O8 and GQD coexisted, the cathodic reduction peak potential of K2S2O8 shifted into a more negative direction at about −1.2V, and produced an intensive cathodic ECL signal at −1.6V, with an onset potential at about −1.0V. The shift of cathodic peak potential was attributed to the adsorption of K2S2O8 on GQD, and the product of K2S2O8 electrochemical reduction contributed to the enhancement of the luminescence of GQD.

The enhanced ECL spectrum was measured for the GQD-K2S2O8 ECL system. The maximum emission wavelength was observed to be about 550nm (see Fig. S4 in the Supporting Information), which corresponds to the emission of the FL spectrum of GQD. The result implied that GQD was the luminant.

Thus, the mechanism in the GQD-K2S2O8 ECL system was speculated as following: peroxydisulfate was absorbed on GQD. Then, peroxydisulfate absorbed on GQD was electrochemically reduced to sulfate free radical SO4 −•. Finally, the free radical SO4 −• oxidized GQD, producing an excited state (GQD*) and then emitting light.

The ECL mechanism with deoxygenating was described as:
S 2
O 8
2 −
+ e − →
SO 4
2 −
+
SO 4
−
•
SO 4
−
• + GQD →
GQD ∗
+
SO 4
2 −
GQD ∗
→ GQD + hv
When the potential was scanned from 0V to −1.6V in the blank PBS (0.1M, pH7.4) solution without deoxygenating, a weak reduction peak appeared at about −0.7V and the reduction current increased while oxygen was inlet (Fig. 4A). This is the electrochemical reduction of dissolved oxygen and H2O2 produced in this process. Moreover, it was found that the ECL signal in GQD-K2S2O8 system after oxygenating was less than that after deoxygenating (see Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information). From these facts, dissolved oxygen was believed also participated in the GQD-K2S2O8 ECL process. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the action nature of dissolved oxygen. For this, H2O2 was used considering that it was the product of electrochemical reduction of dissolved oxygen. Experimental results showed that when H2O2 solutions with different concentrations were added into GQD-K2S2O8 ECL system, the ECL signal gradually decreased with the increasing of the concentration of H2O2 (see Fig. S6 in the Supporting Information). The reason for the decrease of the ECL signal was speculated as following: H2O2 reacted with SO4 −• radicals and SO4 −• radicals were reduced. Only a small amount of residual SO4 −• radicals were left to oxidize GQD to the excited state (GQD*). Therefore, the ECL signal decreased a little. Even so, because as the content of dissolved oxygen in aqueous solution was low and constant, the ECL signal was still strong and very stable (RSD 1.3%) when the signals were measured for 15 continuous cyclic scans in the buffer (see Fig. S7 in the Supporting Information).

The ECL mechanism without deoxygenating is described as:
S 2
O 8
2 −
+ e − →
SO 4
2 −
+
SO 4
−
•
O 2
+ 2 e − + 2
H +
→
H 2
O 2
2
SO 4
−
• +
H 2
O 2
→
O 2
+ 2
SO 4
2 −
SO 4
−
• + GQD →
GQD ∗
+
SO 4
2 −
GQD ∗
→ GQD + hv

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment and characterization of the ECL glucose biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the inhibiting effect of hydrogen peroxide on ECL signal of the GQD-K2S2O8 system and the catalytic effect of GOx, an ECL biosensor for the glucose determination was constructed. Firstly GQD were immobilized on the GCE with chitosan to prepare GQD-chitosan/GCE, then GOx were immobilized by adsorption on the GQD-chitosan/GCE surface to be GOx/GQD-chitosan/GCE. The mechanism of the inhibiting effect of hydrogen peroxide was described in Scheme 1 . When glucose was present, it was catalyzed by GOx on the surface of GQD/chitosan modified GCE to produce gluconic acid and H2O2. Since H2O2 resulted in a quenching effect on the GQD ECL emission, the determination of glucose is accomplished by monitoring the signal-off ECL biosensor.

The construction of the ECL glucose biosensor was characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The impedance measurements of GCE, chitosan/GCE, GQD-chitosan/GCE and GOx/chitosan-GQD/GCE were performed in 5.0mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 solution containing 0.2M KCl. Generally, the semicircular part at higher frequencies of the EIS curve corresponds to the electron-transfer-limited process and its semicircle diameter equals to the electron transfer resistance (Rct). The relationship between Rct and exchange current density is Rct=(RT)/Fi0 [48]. Fig. 5 shows the EIS diagrams of the bare and modified GCEs. The Rct of the chitosan/GCE (1250Ω) was much larger than that of the bare GCE (180Ω), suggesting that a chitosan layer had formed on the electrode surface and also reflecting that the conductive performance of chitosan is not very well. For the GQD-chitosan/GCE, the Rct decreased to 550Ω. Compared with chitosan/GCE, the GQD improved the conductivity and promoted the electron transfer process. When GOx is absorbed, the Rct increased drastically to 2450Ω. This result indicated that the GOx was firmly adsorbed into the GQD–chitosan film, causing an inhibition of the electron transfer of the redox couple.

The voltammetric and ECL performances of bare GCE, GQD/GCE, GQD-chitosan/GCE and GOx/chitosan-GQD/GCE were examined in PBS (0.1M, pH7.4) containing 0.1M K2S2O8. The CV curves were performed in the range of 0–−1.6V with the scan rate of 0.1V·s−1. The ECL potential curves were simultaneously recorded. As showed in Fig. 6A, it can be seen that K2S2O8 showed a reduction peak with different peak potential at all the electrodes. For the ECL signals (Fig. 6B), the ECL peak onset potential was approximate at −1.0V. This is consistent with the ECL property of GQD reported previously [38]. The ECL signal for GCE was the highest and the signals were decreased in different degrees for other electrodes like GOx/chitosan-GQD/GCE and chitosan-GQD/GCE. The reason for the reduction of the ECL signal is presumably due to the decreased conductivity of chitosan and the inhibiting effect of H2O2 produced by glucose by the aid of GOx.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the ECL glucose biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Glucose was determined with the GOx/chitosan-GQD/GCE ECL biosensor. The conditions for glucose determination were optimized. Electrolyte solution was a PBS (0.1M, pH7.4) solution containing 0.1M K2S2O8 and saturated with air. The linear sweep potential scanned from 0 to −1.6V with the scan rate of 0.1V·s−1. As showed in Fig. 7A, the decrement of the ECL intensity was linear correlation to the concentration of glucose in the range of 1.2–120.0pmol·L−1 under the optimum conditions. The linear regression equation was calculated as y=0.0071x+0.1376 (where y and x indicated the ECL inhibition efficiency of I0 −I/I0 and the concentration of glucose, respectively) with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9903 (Fig. 7B).The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.3pmol·L−1. The stability of the GOx/chitosan-GQD/GCE ECL sensor was evaluated by monitoring the response of the ECL emission for consecutive cyclic scans and after different time intervals. The results showed that the ECL intensity of the biosensor kept constantly and the ECL response also remained approximately 96.5% and 92.7% after it was stored 10 and 15days, respectively. The relative standard deviations for glucose (100pmol·L−1) determination using three repeated biosensors were 4.12%. Compared with other electrochemical or ECL glucose biosensors listed in Table 1 , the ECL glucose biosensor in this work showed excellent analysis performances. Therefore, it can be further applied in many fields.

In this work, we have demonstrated that hydrogen peroxide used as the only chemical reagent can convert GO sheets into GQD under the assistance of ultrasound and UV irradiation. The prepared GQD has good electrochemical activity and luminescence properties. Based on the unique ECL characteristics of GQD and the catalysis of GOx, an ECL sensor for the sensitive detection of glucose was developed. This work has some significance for the study of the properties and applications of GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21305111), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (No. 2015M572589) and Scientific Research Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory (No. 15JS099).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2017.07.019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anodic electrochemiluminescence of CdTe quantum dots and its energy transfer for detection of catechol derivatives
